FT_IC1.evidence: U.S. ABCD Study, baseline ages 9-10 across 21 sites, Release 2.0

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical analysis of ABCD data; cross-sectional baseline survey examining associations between screen media use and sleep-wake disturbances

FT_IC6.evidence: Published peer-reviewed paper in Sleep Health, 2020

FT_IC2.evidence: Step 1 finds phone/SM-specific variables: TV use, video, video game, social media, texting, and video chat, each reported separately and paired with individual-trait sleep outcomes (sleep duration, sleep onset latency, insomnia, sleep-wake disturbance severity). Tables 2-3 report direct coefficients and mean differences for each media type.

FT_IC3.evidence: Extensive numeric results: logistic regressions with odds ratios (OR range 1.27-2.41), mean differences, adjusted coefficients throughout Tables 2-3. Example: TV use ≥2 hours vs <2 hours weekday OR=1.27 for sleep duration, OR=1.93 for sleep onset latency

FT_IC4.evidence: All estimates are quantitative and reported for screen media↔sleep outcome relationships across main, supplementary, and sensitivity analyses

FT_EC7_reason: Design is cross-sectional; EC7 applies only to longitudinal SM-as-DV-only papers
notes: Strong paper with clear per-modality screen media estimates paired with individual-trait health outcomes; extensive supplementary results

FT_IC1.evidence: U.S. ABCD Study, ages 9-10 years, n=9254 youth

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical analysis of ABCD data; cross-sectional baseline survey examining associations between screen media activity (SMA) and recreational activity endorsement

FT_IC6.evidence: Published peer-reviewed paper in BMC Public Health, 2020

FT_IC2.evidence: Step 1: No per-modality phone/SM estimates reported as direct coefficients. Step 2 composite check: SMA composite is defined in methods as including "TV shows or movies; videos; video games on a computer, console, phone or other device; messaging on a cell phone, tablet, or computer; social networking sites; video chat." This bundles TV, console/PC gaming, and generic computer use without separate breakouts. Per v7 binding rule: non-phone/non-SM ingredients (TV, console gaming, PC gaming, unspecified gaming, computer-only) without separate breakdown → NOT_MET

notes: Paper uses latent factor analysis with composite SMA variable; does not isolate phone/SM-specific modalities from TV and console/PC gaming

FT_IC1.evidence: U.S. ABCD Study, baseline ages 9-10 from 9986 families, baseline data 2016-2018

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical analysis of ABCD data; cross-sectional study examining relationships between mood symptoms and different types of screen time using network analysis

FT_IC6.evidence: Published peer-reviewed paper in Journal of Abnormal Child Psychology, 2020

FT_IC2.evidence: Step 1 identifies phone/SM-specific screen-time variables analyzed separately: TV/movie, Video, Video game, Texting, Social media, Video chat. Methods report screen time assessed by combining behavioral questions on watching television/movies, videos, video games, social media sites, texting, and video chatting measured on typical weekday/weekend. Results report direct conditional dependencies between each screen-time node and mood symptom nodes (Depressive, Manic-like, Irritability, Anhedonia, Elevated mood, Decreased need for sleep). Key finding: age-inappropriate screen time (mature video games, R-rated movies) shows significant associations with mood symptoms independent from other screen types (Figure 1, Network Analysis section, Figure 2)

FT_IC3.evidence: Numeric results reported throughout: network edges with conditional dependencies, regression coefficients (β), t-statistics, and p-values. Examples from Results: "Mature Video Games: irr=.006, t=2.382, p=.017"; "R-rated Movies: irr=.005, t=7.34, p<.001"; logistic regression coefficients for age-inappropriate content associations with mood symptoms

FT_IC4.evidence: Quantitative results for screen time↔mood outcome relationships reported throughout network analyses, multiple regression tests, and bootstrapped network estimates

FT_EC7_reason: Design is cross-sectional; EC7 applies only to longitudinal SM-as-DV-only papers
notes: Network analysis approach examining separate screen-time modalities paired with individual-trait mood symptom variables; supplementary materials provide detailed network statistics and sex-stratified analyses
